---
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: ["default", "extra.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    seal: false
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: '16:9'
---

```{r, echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE}
##xaringan::inf_mr()
## For offline work: https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown/some-tips.html#working-offline
## Images not appearing? Put images folder inside the libs folder as that is the main data directory

library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(stargazer)
##library(kableExtra)
##library(modelr)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE,
                      eval = TRUE,
                      error = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      comment = NA)
```

class: slideblue

.size80[**Today's Agenda**]

<br>

.size50[
Designing an Environmental Policy

+ "Green" Tax Policies
]

<br>

.center[.size40[
  Justin Leinaweaver (Spring 2022)
]]

???

## Prep for Class
1. Prep email with link to Google sheet for collecting case evidence



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_triangles_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

class: middle

# Paper 3
.size40[
Propose a policy to address your selected environmental problem adapted to the specific features of your problem (Paper 1) and the complications you previously identified (Paper 2).

Support all claims with evidence (e.g. relevant academic literature or real-world examples of this kind of problem being addressed successfully)
]

???

Let's make sure to keep the prompt for your final paper in mind as we explore each of the policy design options.

### With a week's work under our belts on this, any questions on the final paper?

--

.size40[
.textred[**Last Week: Command and Control Regulations**]
]

???

<br>

Last week we explored command and control regulations.

### Sum it up for me, what does it mean to use a command and control approach to addressing an environmental problem?

(SLIDE: Supply and demand diagram)



---

```{r, fig.retina=3, fig.asp=0.618, fig.align='center', out.width='95%', fig.width=6}
## Let's build supply and demand curves
p1 <- tibble(
  Price = 1:100,
  Quantity = 100:1
) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Quantity, y = Price)) +
  geom_point(color = "white") +
  theme_classic() +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 125)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 100, 20)) +
  labs(x = "Quantity Produced", y = "Price per Widget") +
  annotate("segment", x = 0, xend = 100, y = 15, yend = 65, color = "grey", size = 1.3) +
  annotate("segment", x = 0, xend = 100, y = 35, yend = 85, color = "blue", size = 1.3) +
  annotate("segment", x = 0, xend = 100, y = 100, yend = 0, color = "red", size = 1.3) +
  annotate("text", x = 115, y = 5, label = "Demand", size = 5, color = "red", hjust = .5) +
  annotate("segment", x = 56, xend = 56, y = 43, yend = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey") +
  annotate("segment", x = 43, xend = 43, y = 56, yend = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("segment", x = 0, xend = 43, y = 56, yend = 56, linetype = "dashed")

p1 +
  annotate("text", x = 115, y = 85, label = "Supply\n(MC + Regs)", size = 5, color = "blue", hjust = .5)
```

???

Let's go back to our supply and demand curves from last week.

### Refresh my memory, how do these lines help us think about how C&C regulations reduce pollution?

<br>

#### - What does the demand line represent and why is it decreasing?
+ (Only so many people want the product at all and (almost) everyone's desire for it will be sensitive to the price you charge for it.)

#### - What does the supply line represent and why is it increasing?
+ (Assumes marginal costs increase with quantity of product produced, e.g. sourcing more materials for your factory or building new equipment to extract oil from harder to work places, etc.)

#### - So, according to this diagram what happens when we regulate how factories operate?
+ (This diagram does a nice job oversimplifying the trade-offs of the regulatory approach.)
+ We immediately get less pollution in a very controlled way, but
+ Prices go up and supply goes down

<br>

### Bottom line on C&C, what were our takeaways from last week?
#### - Best advice for those who want to use regulations to address an environmental problem?

+ Be careful of economy wide effects (overall effect on the economy is hard to predict, could grow or shrink depending on many other factors involved)
    + Must be paired with assistance for impacted workers?
+ Be careful of political interests in the design (lobbying and/or protection of well connected industries)
+ Regulations have got to keep up with changes in the market and technology (no drift!)
- Depends on balance of "dirty" vs "clean" industries in the target economy
+ C&C most realistic if changes are small OR industries carefully targeted?
+ Enforcement at the individual level is hard if community doesn't buy-in

<br>

### Anybody leaning this way for their third paper?
#### - Regulations are certainly the most straight-forward way to reduce pollution!




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/11-1-Green_taxation_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: top, center

.size60[**Policy Design Option 2: "Green" Taxes**]

???

This week we shift to your next option: "Green" taxation policies

Everybody likes paying higher taxes, right?

+ Let's do it for a good cause!

--

<br>

<br>

<br>

<br>

<br>

<br>

<br>

<br>

.size40[**12.3 Market-Oriented Environmental Tools [(OpenStax 2016)**](https://opentextbc.ca/principlesofeconomics/chapter/12-3-market-oriented-environmental-tools/)]

???

<br>

### Based on the open econ textbook reading, talk me through the basics of using a tax, or "pollution charge," to address an environmental problem.

*next slides* 

1. Fig 1 from textbook, and 

2. Supply/demand curve for comparing to C&C



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/11-1-OET-Fig1.jpg')
background-size: 90%
background-position: center

???

### What in the world is this meant to show me?
+ Figure 1 illustrates the marginal costs that a firm faces in reducing pollution
+ Increases with output because "the firm can use the cheapest and easiest method to make initial reductions in pollution, but additional reductions in pollution become more expensive."
    + First 10lb emissions costs $300 to eliminate
    + Second 10lb costs $500

+ Hypothetical pollution tax of $1,000 per 10lb of emissions
    + Cheaper to reduce pollution for 10, 20 and 30lb so do that
    + Cheaper to pay the tax for the 40 and 50lb amounts so do that

+ So, $1k tax reduces this firms emissions by 30lb BUT the firm gets to produce as many widgets as it wants (assuming it can sell them!)

#### - Make sense?
+ Unlike the C&C regulations, the tax approach means we get pollution cuts of different levels by different companies.
    + Much more variation in terms of stuff in the market, prices to be paid BUT probably higher pollution!

<br>

### Real-world examples of pollution charges in my life (from the chapter)?
+ (Taxes on gasoline to reduce air pollution and pay for road upkeep)
+ (Pay for garbage pickup, especially with a "pay as you throw" policy to incentivize less waste production at the household level)

#### - How does a gasoline tax impact me like this chart does for a polluting industry. Connect these dots for me.

+ Assuming the gas tax exists in MO to reduce pollution, it probably doesn't almost at all.

+ MO is currently one of the lowest in the country (19.5 cents / gallon) and that is nowhere near the price necessary for me to reconsider driving to work.

#### - How high would our gas tax have to be to get you to opt out of driving more often?

<br>

Let's go back to our supply and demand curves.



---

```{r, fig.retina=3, fig.asp=0.618, fig.align='center', out.width='95%', fig.width=6}
p1 +
  annotate("text", x = 115, y = 85, label = "Supply\n(MC + Tax)", size = 5, color = "blue", hjust = .5)
```

???

To keep things simple, AND WILDLY OVERSIMPLIFIED, I've simply changed the supply label to refer to a green tax rather than a regulation requiring new technology be installed.

In this hypothetical, the government has applied a tax to production by industries that emit a certain pollution type and indexed it to the amount they produce.

This tax increases the MC as before so the industry produces fewer widgets and less pollution.

<br>

This has many of the same drawbacks as before, however unlike the regulation, the tax is also a dynamic incentive to innovate!

### Why is a green tax more likely to produce innovation than a C&C regulation?

(SLIDE)


---

```{r, fig.retina=3, fig.asp=0.618, fig.align='center', out.width='95%', fig.width=6, cache=TRUE}
## Let's build supply and demand curves that move with innovation!
## Have to install transformr for lines moving
library(gganimate)

d1 <- tibble(
  Quantity = 1:100,
  Supply1 = 42 + .5*Quantity,
  Supply2 = 18 + .5*Quantity,
  Demand = 100 - 1*Quantity
) |>
  pivot_longer(cols = Supply1:Supply2, names_to = "Scenario", values_to = "Value")

d1 |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Quantity)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = Demand), color = "red", size = 1.2) +
  geom_line(aes(y = Value), color = "blue", size = 1.2) +
  theme_classic() +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 125)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 100, 20)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 100, 20), limits = c(0, 100)) +
  labs(x = "Quantity Produced", y = "Price per Widget",
       title = "Green Taxes: Innovation can lower the MC") +
  annotate("text", x = 115, y = 5, label = "Demand", size = 5, color = "red", hjust = .5) +
  annotate("text", x = 119, y = 85, label = "Marginal\nCost", size = 5, color = "blue", hjust = .5) +
  transition_states(Scenario, wrap=FALSE) +
  shadow_wake(wake_length = .5, colour = "grey")
```

???

Designing a tax means using the power of the market to reduce pollution.

Markets, as all good capitalists know, move MUCH faster and more efficiently to produce profits than top-down regulators possibly can.

The key is that the tax makes finding a way to pollute less during production a profit-maximizing decision.

If you make "being green" profitable then the market will take care of it for you.

<br>

Why can we be confident in this progress for our economy?

Frankly speaking, the real world is much more complex than the diagram let's us easily see.

+ All firms have different MC curves (e.g pollution abatement costs) and so the easiest change is simply to advantage companies that already exist with current tech!

+ Technology is constantly evolving and money spent on abatement now tends to make abatement later even cheaper.

<br>

Bottom line-ish, using a "green" tax means trusting the market to embrace innovation to reduce pollution.

Clearly there's way more to the economics of this and for that I encourage you to take Environmental Econ!

# Optional: Textbook chapter also introduces additional market mechanisms (cap and trade) and property rights approaches (Coase theorem) if you want to introduce them

Otherwise:

For us, we need to dig into the politics!




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/11-1-heritage.png')
background-size: 85%
background-position: center

???

Let's start with the opposition from the right.

### What are the key premises in the argument made by Loris and Williamson (2019) from the Heritage Foundation?

+ (?)

<br>

### How convincing do you find this argument?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/11-1-Energy_Justice_Network.png')
background-size: 95%
background-position: center

???

And now some opposition from the left!

### What are the key premises in the argument made by Ewall from the Energy Justice Network?

+ (?)

<br>

### How convincing do you find this argument?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/11-1-carbon-tax-plans-compared.png')
background-size: 50%
background-position: left
class: middle, center, slideblue

.pull-right[
.size40[
The Energy Innovation and Carbon Dividend Act ([Link](https://energyinnovationact.org/))

<br>

The Baker Shultz Carbon Dividends Plan: Bipartisan Climate Roadmap ([Link](https://clcouncil.org/Bipartisan-Climate-Roadmap.pdf))
]]

???

Let's review some policy options for the US.

+ Lavelle 2019 ([Link](https://insideclimatenews.org/news/07032019/carbon-tax-proposals-compare-baker-shultz-exxon-conocophillips-ccl-congress/)) Reviews two competing carbon tax plans 

### If you had to pick one, which way do you lean and why?
+ Discuss

### Did anybody dislike both? Why?
+ Discuss



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size50[**Thursday: "Green" Taxation Cases**]

.size40[
Find a real world example of this approach being used to address an environmental problem similar to the one in your project.

+ "Similar" in ANY of the specifics OR complications in your problem

+ What is the lesson for our new policy designs? 
]

???
